title: Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the TSA SD programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with TSA SD control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for III.A.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that TSA SD III.A (Cybersecurity plan) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.16.18: Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.16.18' running on pan:traffic, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, nozomi:alert, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper TSA SD control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the TSA SD control objective.

dataSources: `pan:traffic`, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, `nozomi:alert`, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery
app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Industrial Asset Intelligence, Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (4295), Splunk Edge Hub, Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:problem category="OT Cyber Incident" earliest=-365d
| eval uc_tag="22.16.18"
| eval ok=if(match(work_notes,"(?i)restored") AND match(work_notes,"(?i)tested"),1,0)
| where ok=0 AND state="Closed"
| table number, short_description, assignment_group
description: Supports pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records".
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for TSA SD compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:problem. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=itsm`.
visualization: Time chart, Choropleth or site bar, Table, Single value. Key fields: `assignment_group`, `number`, `ok`, `short_description`, `uc_tag`.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `III.A`. The control objective is: "Evidence that TSA SD III.A (Cybersecurity plan) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.16.18: Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:problem`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

This search aggregates `count()` across the last year, grouped by `the analytical dimensions`. For "Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of TSA SD control activity. The SPL tags each event with `uc_tag="22.16.18"` — these constants are the detection's identity signature; any downstream search ('alert all of one detection family,' 'evidence for clause X') can filter on these constants instead of re-deriving from the full base search. The aggregation approach is chosen to produce one result row per analytical unit, which is the typical requirement for compliance dashboarding and report generation.

Why aggregate at search time rather than rely on raw events: compliance reporting requires summarisation to be defensible and stable. A raw-event dashboard can show different counts depending on the exact second it was queried; an aggregated result rounded to the UC's schedule produces consistent numbers across the reporting period. If the downstream consumer (GRC platform, regulator submission) expects stable daily/weekly figures, schedule this search to write to a summary index and read from the summary for reports — that way the numbers quoted on Monday match what was quoted on Wednesday.

Cardinality vigilance: if `the analytical dimensions` has very high cardinality (e.g. grouping by a free-text `user_agent` or `url` field), the result set explodes and the search becomes slow. Check cardinality with `| stats dc(the analytical dimensions) as cardinality` before scheduling; if >100000 distinct groups, either narrow the search, pre-filter to the top N values, or redesign the search to aggregate at a coarser grain.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.16.18: Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -365d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.16.18,reg=TSA_SD
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:problem earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `assignment_group`, `number`, `ok`, `short_description`, `uc_tag`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:problem earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table assignment_group, number, ok, short_description, uc_tag
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.16.18: TSA SD evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 2 — Choropleth Or Site Bar (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (the key fields):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'TSA SD current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**TSA SD-specific failure modes:**

• Covered surface transportation: TSA SDs cover specific rail, pipeline, and transit operators. Detections must scope to covered operations; non-covered assets are noise.

• Cybersecurity Coordinator designation: each covered entity has a designated CC. Detections on IR must route through the CC's communication channel; bypassing the CC is a procedural finding.

• CISA reporting timelines: different incident types have different reporting windows (e.g. 24h for certain classes). Detections on reporting SLA must encode the specific window per incident type.

• OT/IT segmentation: TSA SDs strongly emphasise OT/IT separation. Detections on cross-domain access must identify which side is which; an alert that doesn't know its physical/cyber domain is ambiguous.
grandmaExplanation: We support pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "Post-incident recovery test evidence in problem records". That lines up with pipeline security expectations and helps the team show its work to regulators and leadership.
